spoken_input:
Then the judgment becomes a schedule decision. The language changes again. It is no longer mainly about erosion depth or sealing time. It is about whether there is enough cause to delay. Delay has a phone tree. Delay has cost. Delay has a visible owner.

If the flight succeeds, the decision becomes precedent. We flew with that condition and came back. That fact starts to compete with the warning that preceded it.

This route ran more than once before Challenger. Each pass made the next one easier to defend.

Engineers were not simply ignored. They were often heard, then absorbed into a process that had learned how to carry their concern without stopping.

Months before Challenger, Roger Boisjoly warned that a similar field-joint problem could lead to catastrophe of the highest order and loss of human life.

That memo did not come from hindsight. It came from inside the system, before the accident, from an engineer close to the hardware.

The temperature pattern also had more weight than the launch-night argument made it feel. When the full flight history was framed one way, the cold-weather signal could look debatable. When the full record was framed another way, cold O-ring distress became much harder to dismiss.

A chart can contain real information and still weaken the warning if it frames the wrong question.

Richard Feynman's later appendix named the broader danger plainly: previous success can become misleading evidence. A survived flight can show that the vehicle got through. It does not prove the risk was safe to repeat.

By January 1986, Challenger was not facing a new problem.

The program had evidence of O-ring distress. It had temperature concern. It had waivers. It had memos. It had a known field-joint problem. It had a launch system that kept treating survival as part of the safety case.

Then the weather turned that old problem into a launch decision.

January 27, 1986. Less than twenty-four hours before the scheduled launch.

A teleconference connected NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center, Kennedy Space Center, and Morton Thiokol, the contractor that built the solid rocket boosters.

The subject was temperature. Forecasts and engineering estimates pointed to launch conditions far colder than prior Shuttle experience. The launch temperature would be about 36 degrees Fahrenheit. The right aft field joint was estimated colder, around 28 degrees Fahrenheit, plus or minus five degrees.

previous_text:
e discussion becomes a judgment call. Does this affect the next flight? The answer depends on the question the room decides to ask.

If the question is, can we prove this will cause a failure, the answer is usually no. Predicting a specific loss on a specific flight is hard. If the question is, can we prove this will not fail, the answer is also no. But the first question keeps the schedule alive.
next_text:
Thiokol engineers were concerned because cold could slow the O-rings at the exact moment they needed to seal.

Their recommendation was clear: do not launch below 53 degrees Fahrenheit. That number mattered because it was tied to the lowest relevant prior experience they believed they could defend.
